text:
The weekend, that mythical golden era of human freedom, once again delivered its signature performance: approximately 48 hours of relative autonomy followed by an abrupt and frankly rude cancellation of all good things. Critics are calling it "another masterpiece of brevity" and "the most anticipated disappointment of the week."

Friday evening opens with tremendous promise. The pacing is exhilarating, the mood is euphoric, and audiences everywhere report feeling something approaching genuine joy. Saturday morning sustains this energy admirably, with a bold extended-sleep sequence that many viewers found to be the emotional highlight of the entire production. Saturday afternoon, however, begins to introduce subtle tension as the shadow of Sunday evening looms on the horizon like a storm cloud with a very specific arrival time.

Sunday is where the weekend truly reveals its character, or rather its lack of one. The morning is gorgeous but haunted. The afternoon collapses into a kind of existential dread that philosophers have yet to adequately name, though "the Sunday scaries" is doing its best. By evening the whole enterprise has the atmosphere of a farewell party where someone keeps reminding everyone of the exact minute they have to leave.

The runtime is the most glaring flaw. Forty-eight hours is simply insufficient for an audience that spent the preceding 120 hours earning the right to enjoy it. The producers have inexplicably scheduled it between two five-day stretches of obligation, which suggests either poor planning or a fundamental hostility toward the audience. The weekend shows up late, leaves early, and somehow charges full price.

It remains highly recommended, of course, because the alternative is having no weekend at all, which the producers know perfectly well. Four stars, would suffer through again.
